exercises.title: Rewrite the Bad Email
exercises.description: Rewrite this poorly written email using the 4 C's (Clear, Concise, Correct, Courteous).
hints: Start with a specific subject line
State the purpose in the first sentence
Use bullet points for multiple items
End with a clear call to action and deadline
exercises.original:
Subject: Hi
Hey, so I was just thinking about the project and wanted to reach out to see how things are going and maybe we could set up a time to chat about the budget stuff because there are some things I need to go over and I think it would be good to connect soon if possible, no rush though. Also, the designs are looking pretty good I guess but there are some changes that need to be made but I'll tell you about those when we talk. Thanks!
exercises.solution:
Subject: Project Budget Review — Meeting Request

Hi [Name],

I'd like to schedule 30 minutes to review the Q3 budget allocations.

Key topics:
• Budget reallocation between marketing and engineering
• Design feedback (3 specific changes needed)
• Timeline for final approval

Are you available Tuesday or Wednesday afternoon? Please let me know what works.

Thanks,
[Your name]

What changed: Specific subject, purpose in the first line, organized topics, clear call to action with options.

exercises.title: The Professional 'No' Draft
exercises.description: Write a professional email declining each of these requests. Be direct but courteous.
hints: Give a clear, brief reason (not an excuse)
Don't apologize excessively — one 'sorry' is enough
scenarios: A colleague asks you to join a committee that would take 5 hours/week
A client asks for a 30% discount on your standard pricing
Your manager asks you to work on a weekend project
exercises.solution: Example 1: 'Thanks for thinking of me for the committee. Unfortunately, I can't commit to 5 hours weekly right now — my current project load doesn't allow it. I'd be happy to contribute to specific topics as needed, or recommend a colleague who might be a good fit.' Example 2: 'Thank you for your interest in our services. Our pricing reflects the value and support we provide. I'd be happy to discuss a scaled package that fits your budget.' Example 3: 'I understand the urgency. I have a family commitment this weekend. I can prioritize this first thing Monday morning, or [colleague] could assist Saturday. Which works better?'

exercises.title: The Email Audit
exercises.description: Review your last 5 sent emails. Score each one on these criteria (1-5 scale):
hints: Be honest — most of us score lower than we think
Look for patterns across your emails
Pick your lowest-scoring area and focus on improving it this week
criteria: Subject line: Does it tell the reader what's needed and by when?
Opening: Does the first sentence state the purpose?
Structure: Is it scannable (bullet points, short paragraphs)?
Call to action: Is there a clear next step with a deadline?
Tone: Does it sound professional and warm?
exercises.solution: After scoring, most people find their weakest area is either the subject line (too vague) or the call to action (missing or unclear). This week, focus on adding a specific deadline and action to every email you send. Small improvements compound — after 2 weeks, structured writing becomes automatic.
